article.title: Stanford AI Index 2026 finds US-China model gap compressed to 2.7 percent as researcher migration to America collapses
article.summary: Stanford HAI's ninth annual AI Index puts the top US model only 2.7 percent ahead of China's best, reports an 89 percent drop in AI talent moving to the US since 2017, and flags widening gaps in model transparency and safety evaluation.
article.body_markdown:
## Overview

Stanford's Institute for Human-Centered Artificial Intelligence (HAI) published the ninth edition of its annual AI Index this week, and its headline finding is that the performance gap between the top American and Chinese large language models has effectively collapsed. The report documents a year in which Chinese models repeatedly traded the top spot with US systems on public benchmarks, private AI investment hit new highs in both countries, and the long-running flow of AI researchers into the United States slowed to a trickle.

## What the Report Says About the US-China Gap

According to [Fortune](https://fortune.com/2026/04/16/stanford-study-how-has-china-gained-on-us-ai-war/), the Stanford HAI 2026 AI Index finds that in May 2023 OpenAI's GPT-4 led the Chatbot Arena leaderboard with more than 1,300 Arena points while the best Chinese model scored fewer than 1,000. By March 2026 that gulf had shrunk to 39 Arena points, with Anthropic's Claude Opus 4.6 ahead of China's Dola-Seed 2.0 by just 2.7 percent on the benchmarks Stanford tracks.

Fortune reports that models from the two countries have "traded the lead multiple times since early 2025," and that Stanford concluded China "gradually gaining ground, and this year it appears to have nearly erased any U.S. lead."

The picture is uneven across categories. [Fortune](https://fortune.com/2026/04/16/stanford-study-how-has-china-gained-on-us-ai-war/) notes the US still produces more top-tier models — 50 notable systems in 2025 versus 30 from China — and leads on high-impact patents, while China holds a larger share of global AI citations at 20.6 percent versus 12.6 percent for the US in 2024, and installed roughly nine times more industrial robots last year (295,000 versus 34,200).

## Investment, Adoption, and Infrastructure

Private investment figures underline how differently the two AI superpowers are resourced. [SiliconANGLE](https://siliconangle.com/2026/04/13/stanford-hais-2026-ai-index-reveals-china-u-s-now-neck-neck-race-global-dominance/) reports that US private AI investment reached $285.9 billion in 2025, approximately 23 times China's $12.4 billion, and that 1,953 newly funded AI companies were launched in the US during the year. The outlet notes that HAI researchers caution these private figures likely understate Chinese activity because government guidance funds have deployed an estimated $184 billion into AI firms between 2000 and 2023.

On adoption, [SiliconANGLE](https://siliconangle.com/2026/04/13/stanford-hais-2026-ai-index-reveals-china-u-s-now-neck-neck-race-global-dominance/) summarizes the Index's finding that generative AI has reached 53 percent of the global population within three years of widespread availability, outpacing the adoption curves of the personal computer and the internet. The same source reports that 44 countries now operate state-backed supercomputing clusters, and that corporate AI investment has risen 40-fold since 2013.

## The Talent Flow Has Reversed

One of the most striking data points in the report concerns researcher migration. [Fortune](https://fortune.com/2026/04/16/stanford-study-how-has-china-gained-on-us-ai-war/) cites Stanford's analysis showing that the number of AI scholars relocating to the United States has fallen 89 percent since 2017, with 80 percent of that decline occurring in just the past year. Fortune quotes a Hoover Institution report referenced in the Index that "most returned to China, creating a 'one-way knowledge transfer' in China's favor."

## Transparency and Safety Are Not Keeping Up

Beyond the geopolitical framing, the Index documents a widening gap between what frontier models can do and how well the public can evaluate them. [SiliconANGLE](https://siliconangle.com/2026/04/13/stanford-hais-2026-ai-index-reveals-china-u-s-now-neck-neck-race-global-dominance/) reports that "80 of the 95 most notable models launched last year were released without their training code," and that major labs including Google, Anthropic, and OpenAI have stopped disclosing dataset sizes and training duration for their newest systems.

The same outlet reports that trust in government regulation of AI is weakest in the country that produces the most frontier models: the US ranks last among the countries surveyed, with only 31 percent of respondents saying they trust their government to regulate AI responsibly.

## What We Don't Know

The Index's 2.7 percent US lead is a single snapshot on one family of public benchmarks. Stanford's own methodology note, as summarized by [Fortune](https://fortune.com/2026/04/16/stanford-study-how-has-china-gained-on-us-ai-war/), acknowledges that benchmark leadership has changed hands repeatedly since early 2025 and says little about proprietary or classified capabilities that neither country discloses. The Index also does not assess the impact of US export controls on advanced chips, which sit in the background of every comparison between American and Chinese model development.

It is likewise unclear how long the near-parity will persist. [SiliconANGLE](https://siliconangle.com/2026/04/13/stanford-hais-2026-ai-index-reveals-china-u-s-now-neck-neck-race-global-dominance/) notes that the US still leads on capital, data-center capacity, and semiconductor fabrication, while China leads on research volume and robotics deployment — a split that could widen again in either direction depending on compute availability and regulatory decisions now working through US and Chinese governments.
